coredata.dc:title: CD-LRH@MnO2 fluorescent / colorimetric dual-mode sensor for the detection of ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Er, Xinyu
Gu, Qingyang
Niu, Zhaoqian
Sun, Jia
Ding, Zijun
Liang, Shuang
Jin, Haibo
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent / colorimetric sensor was established for the dual-mode detection of ascorbic acid (AA). Carbon quantum dots (CD) was intercalated into layered europium hydroxide (LEuH), and then delaminated into rare-earth nanosheets. The rare-earth nanosheets were combined with MnO2 nanosheets by electrostatic assembly to obtain the CD-LEuH@MnO2. MnO2 can quench the fluorescence of CD-LEuH, however, the presence of AA can reduce MnO2 in CD-LEuH@MnO2 to manganese ions, leading to fluorescence recovery. In addition, MnO2 shows excellent oxidase-like activity, which can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the absence of H2O2, promoting the establishment of a colorimetric detection system. The limit of detection (LOD) of fluorescence and colorimetric detection was 0.081 μM and 0.064 μM, respectively. Meanwhile, the sensing system shows excellent performance in the actual samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent / colorimetric sensor
MnO2
Layered rare-earth hydroxide
Carbon dots
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and characterization of synthetic materials
Quenching mechanisms of MnO2 to CD-LEuH
Sensing mechanisms of CD-LEuH@MnO2 for AA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image and Elemental mapping image of CD-LEuH@MnO2; (B) XRD patterns of Cl-LEuH (a), CD-LEuH (b), MnO2 (c); (C) FT-IR spectra of Glu (a), OPD (b), CD (c), Cl-LEuH (d), CD-LEuH (e), MnO2 (f) and CD-LEuH@MnO2 (g); (D) XPS survey spectrum of MnO2; High-resolution scans for Mn 2p (E) electrons of MnO2.

Study of the optimal volume ratio of CD-LEuH to MnO2 (A); Fluorescence response to different concentrations of AA (B); AA standard equation for fluorescence detection (5–30 μM) (C); CIE chromaticity coordinates of CD-LEuH@MnO2 with different AA concentrations (D); CD-LEuH to MnO2 UV colorimetric detection of different concentrations of AA spectra (E); AA standard equation for UV colorimetric detection (0.5–4.5 μM) (F).

The fluorescence excitation spectra, the fluorescence emission spectra of CD – LEuH, and the UV–Vis absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets (A); Optimal excitation wavelength study of CD-LEuH@MnO2 for AA detection (B); Quenching efficiency of observed and corrected (E obsd and E cor) (C).
CD-LEuH@MnO2 fluorescence lifetime(A); CD-LEuH@MnO2 fluorescence lifetime after addition of AA (B).
CD-LEuH@MnO2 is resistant to interference in fluorescence detection (A) and UV colorimetric detection (B).
Parameters for the effect of IFE of MnO2 on CD-LEuH fluorescence.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: E obsd : Observed suppression efficiency, E obsd = 1 − F obsd/F obsd,0.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: E cor : Calibrated suppression efficiency, E cor = 1 − F cor/F cor,0.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All samples are averaged over three measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of rare-earth probes for AA detection.

head.ce:title: CD-LRH@MnO2 fluorescent / colorimetric dual-mode sensor for the detection of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing 102617, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing 102617, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology, Beijing 102617, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology, Beijing 102617, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing 102617, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent / colorimetric sensor was established for the dual-mode detection of ascorbic acid (AA). Carbon quantum dots (CD) was intercalated into layered europium hydroxide (LEuH), and then delaminated into rare-earth nanosheets. The rare-earth nanosheets were combined with MnO2 nanosheets by electrostatic assembly to obtain the CD-LEuH@MnO2. MnO2 can quench the fluorescence of CD-LEuH, however, the presence of AA can reduce MnO2 in CD-LEuH@MnO2 to manganese ions, leading to fluorescence recovery. In addition, MnO2 shows excellent oxidase-like activity, which can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the absence of H2O2, promoting the establishment of a colorimetric detection system. The limit of detection (LOD) of fluorescence and colorimetric detection was 0.081 μM and 0.064 μM, respectively. Meanwhile, the sensing system shows excellent performance in the actual samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), a significant trace element, is of great significance in the physiological functions of the human body [1,2]. It can increase immune activity, and prevent or treat certain diseases, such as colds, urinary stones, and cancer [3,4]. Currently, many assays have been developed for detecting AA, such as fluorescence spectrometry, electrochemical assay, colorimetric assay, chromatographic assay, and so on [5–7]. Despite continuous advancements in AA detection technologies, challenges persist [8,9]. For example, AA is prone to oxidation in the environment, necessitating rapid analysis. Actual samples often contain interfering substances that can disrupt the detection process. Single-mode sensors, which provide only one output signal, are less accurate compared to dual-signal sensors that offer two output signals.

Two-dimensional materials (2D) have high surface areas, providing more detection sites. Manganese dioxide (MnO2) and layered rare-earth hydroxide (LRH) are widely used as typical 2D nanomaterials. The presence of +2, +3, or +4 valence states of Mn in MnO2 nanostructures, as well as the presence of two or three valence states in the same crystal structure, endowing MnO2 with unique properties in physical, chemical, and electrochemical aspects, etc [10]. The layered manganese dioxide host layer and interlayer cations are bound together through electrostatic attraction. Layered manganese dioxide can undergo wash-assisted exfoliation, with the thickness of the exfoliated nanosheets remaining nearly constant. Furthermore, the exfoliated monolayer manganese dioxide can reassemble with positively charged materials under specific conditions to form various composite materials. The property of MnO2 also possesses excellent biocompatibility, which can be degraded to non-toxic manganese ions by the reducing substances in organisms. MnO2 has a broad absorption band at 200–600 nm, and its excellent light absorption allows it to be used as a quencher to quench fluorescent substances to construct fluorescent sensors [11–13]. The mesoporous or hollow structure of MnO2 provides a large specific surface area, which enhances the ability to load drugs and lays the foundation for biomedical applications [14,15]. Moreover, MnO2 exhibits oxidase-like activity, which catalyzes the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the absence of H2O2, producing obvious color changes [16–19]. Therefore, this property can be utilized for colorimetric detection of biomolecules.

The LRH was composed of a positively charged host and a negatively charged interlayer guest [20,21]. The inorganic host can protect the luminescence of the interlayer florescence molecules, producing novel luminescent properties [22,23]. Carbon dots (CD) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials characterized by easy synthesis, structural diversity, high optical properties, low cytotoxicity, good water solubility, and ease of decoration [24–26]. CD can be functionalized by a large number of carboxyl or hydroxyl groups, exhibiting good biocompatibility [27,28].

When CD is introduced into the interlayer, the laminar structure of LRH can provide secondary protection to CD, thus realizing the slow release of the guest molecule. In addition, the hydroxyl group on the CD molecule can combine with the interlayer anion or laminate through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, etc., so as to stably exist in the interlayer. The hydrophilic hydroxyl group on the outside of the CD can also increase the dispersibility of the LRH in polar solvents, reduce agglomeration, and decrease the difficulty of stripping [27].

Currently, CD are combined with 2D materials by in-situ synthesis as well as composite methods. The advantage of in-situ synthesis is that CD can be arranged orderly in the interlayers and tightly bonded through chemical bonds, which helps improve the stability of the material [29]. By contrast, the composite method has the advantage of quick bonding and a reversible process. Moreover, this method has little influence on the internal structure and active centers of the CD and the LRH. Therefore, the combination of CD and LRH can maintain their chemical activities, realizing the superposition and synergistic effect of the two functions [24,27,30].

In this paper, the oxidase-like activity of MnO2 as well as the absorption and fluorescence properties of CD and LRH were utilized to develop a simple, accurate, and efficient dual-mode sensor (CD-LEuH@MnO2) (Scheme 1 ). This sensing platform integrates a fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensing system, enabling self-verification and delivering higher accuracy. CD was intercalated into layered europium hydroxide (LEuH), and then delaminated into rare-earth nanosheets. The rare-earth nanosheets were combined with MnO2 nanosheets by electrostatic assembly. AA can reduce the MnO2 to manganese ions, and the fluorescence was restored by the release of CD-LEuH. The large specific surface area of nanosheets provided more detection sites to enhance the sensitivity.

O-Phenylenediamine (OPD) (2.5 mmol) and glutamic acid (Glu) (2.8 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water by sonication, then transferred to an autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 8 h. The product was cooled to room temperature and subsequently filtered.

EuCl3·6H2O (1 mmol), hexamethylenetetramine (HMT) (1 mmol), and NaCl (13 mmol) were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water, and then reacted in an autoclave at 90 °C for 12 h. After being cooled at room temperature, the substances were centrifuged, washed, and dried in a vacuum at 40 °C for 12 h to obtain the precursor Cl-LEuH.

Cl-LEuH (0.2 g) and sodium 1-octanesulfonate (OS) (2.7 mmol) were dispersed into 60 mL of deionized water, then added 10 mL of CD solution and stirred for 6 h in a 35 °C water bath. The product was filtered, washed, and dried to obtain the composited CD-LEuH.

MnO2 nanosheets were prepared by referring to the current published literature [31]. Tetramethylammonium hydroxide pentahydrate (TMA∙OH) (1 mol/L,12 mL), H2O2 (30 %, 2 mL), and deionized water (20 mL) were thoroughly mixed. Subsequently, MnCl2·4H2O solution (0.3 mol/L, 10 mL) was added dropwise. Following 12 h stirring at ambient temperature, the sample underwent centrifugation, washing, and subsequent drying at 60 °C.
CD-LEuH (20 mg) was dispersed in a mixture of 50 mL of formamide and deionized water (formamide: deionized water = 3:2), which was delaminated at 45 °C for 12 h to obtain a CD-LEuH nanosheets.
MnO2 (20 mg) was dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water, and then sonication-assisted delaminated for 10 h to obtain MnO2 nanosheets.
The CD-LEuH nanosheets were added to poly (allylamine hyclrochloride) (PAH) and mixed with MnO2 nanosheets at different volume ratios to obtain the CD-LEuH@MnO2.

Fluorescence detection: Different concentrations of AA (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 µM) were individually introduced into CD-LEuH@MnO2 solution (1 mL), and mixed thoroughly. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra were measured within the wavelength range from 330 nm to 600 nm under the excitation of 275 nm.

Colorimetric detection: TMB (1 mmol/L, 200 µL) was mixed with CD-LEuH@MnO2 (200 µL). Subsequently, different concentrations of AA solutions (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, 4, and 4.5 µM) were added, and mixed thoroughly. Finally, the UV spectra were measured within the wavelength range of 350–500 nm.

For the actual samples tested in this study, with the exception of the tap water sample which was analyzed directly, all other samples were centrifuged and subsequently diluted 100-fold prior to analysis. The detection method was identical to that described in section 2.6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and characterization of synthetic materials
ce:section.ce:para: The scanning electron microscope (SEM) results reveal that the synthesized CD-LEuH@MnO2 composite displays a three-dimensional wrinkled surface morphology (Fig. 1 A), attributable to the TMA+-intercalated structure of the layered MnO2. Furthermore, EDS confirms the presence of C, O, S, Eu, and Mn elements in the synthesized sample, with homogeneous distribution of these elements throughout the material (Fig. S1).

From the XRD (Fig. 1B), it can be seen that the synthesized material has sharp and symmetric diffraction peaks, indicating a well-crystallized layered compound [20]. Cl-LEuH showed the diffraction at 0.84 and 0.43 nm with a basal spacing (d basal) of 0.84 nm. After the intercalation, the interlayer spacing expanded to 2.58 nm, indicating the successful synthesis of CD-LEuH. The XRD patterns of MnO2 with (001) and (002) crystal planes indicated that layered MnO2 was prepared, and (100) and (110) crystal planes corresponded to the hexagonal 2D crystal cells [32,33].

Fig. 1C shows the FT-IR spectra, and the broad band at 3000–3500 cm−1 was the stretching vibration band of O–H and N–H. For CD (Fig. 1Cc), C=O and C–N stretching vibration was at 1684 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1, respectively. For Cl-LEuH (Fig. 1Cd), the stretching vibration of Eu–O was at 625 and 541 cm−1 [22,23]. For CD-LEuH (Fig. 1Ce), the absorption of C–O was at 1625 cm−1 and 1159 cm−1, and 1106 cm−1 was corresponded to the absorption of
S
O
3
-
in OS. For MnO2 and CD-LEuH@MnO2, the absorption of Mn–O was at 500–600 cm−1 [6].

Elemental composition and corresponding valence states in MnO2 was characterized by XPS (Fig. 1D–E). The two main peaks in the high-resolution maps were attributed to Mn3+ and Mn4+ in the Mn 2p3/2 as well as Mn 2p1/2 orbitals, respectively [12,34]. The higher content of Mn3+, the more oxygen vacancies were found in the prepared MnO2 structure.

The volume ratio of CD-LEuH to MnO2 results showed (Fig. 2 A) that the fluorescence recovery was optimal when the volume ratio was 1:2, so the subsequent experiments were performed using CD-LEuH: MnO2 = 1:2. The fluorescence intensity of CD-LEuH@MnO2 at 364 nm exhibited a continuous increment concomitant with the increase of AA concentration (Fig. 2B). The fluorescence color changed from yellow to blue (Fig. 2D). In the concentration range of 5–30 μM, a good linear relationship was observed (Fig. 2C), with the linear equation of y = 0.1598x − 0.4676 (R 2 = 0.9976). Based on the formula LOD = 3σ/k, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.081 μM.

A UV colorimetric detection AA system was constructed using TMB as the chromogen. In this system, the UV absorbance decreased with increasing concentration of AA (Fig. 2E). Within the concentration range from 0.5 to 4.5 μM, an excellent linear correlation was noted (Fig. 2F). The equation was y = −0.1871x + 0.8369 (R 2 = 0.9904). Additionally, the calculated LOD was 0.064 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanisms of MnO2 to CD-LEuH
ce:section.ce:para:
There was an overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of MnO2 and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CD-LEuH. (Fig. 3 A), thus serving as a potential energy acceptor for quenching the fluorescence of CD-LEuH by Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). From Fig. 3B, the optimal excitation wavelength of CD-LEuH was 275 nm. To further determine the quenching mechanisms, a correction was made using Eq. (1) by considering the cuvette geometry for fluorescence measurements (Fig. S2), CD-LEuH, and the absorption properties of the MnO2 nanosheets [35–37].
(1)
F cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d A e x
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s A e m

The details of the formulas were shown in the Support Information. Table 1 showed the calculated data for different ratios. For the observed and corrected fluorescence intensities for different ratios (Fig. 3C), the inhibitory effect of IFE on CD-LEuH was minimal. Therefore, the quenching of CD-LEuH by MnO2 in the system mainly originated from FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanisms of CD-LEuH@MnO2 for AA
ce:section.ce:para: AA can be detected by redox reaction due to the olefinic diol groups in its molecular structure, which shows strong reducing properties [6]. In the presence of AA, MnO2 was reduced to Mn2+, leading to the release of CD-LEuH and the restoration of fluorescence, and AA was oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA). In addition, the fluorescence lifetime during the detection of AA by MnO2 was investigated, and it was found that the fluorescence lifetime before and after the detection was unchanged significantly to 1230 μs (Fig. 4 ). Therefore, the mechanism of AA detection by MnO2 is dominated by the redox reaction, accompanied by the static quenching phenomenon.

TMB can be enzymatically oxidized to oxTMB by nanozyme materials in the absence of H2O2 [38,39]. MnO2 has oxidase-like activity, which can oxidize TMB to the final yellow diamine product (oxTMB) under acidic conditions, producing characteristic peaks at 450 nm. Meanwhile, during the reaction, MnO2 itself is reduced to generate manganese ions, which further affects the oxidation of TMB, thus decreasing the absorbance at 450 nm [5,17].

Several amino acids, inorganic ions that may be present in the water samples, and substances that may coexist were introduced into this sensor system to be used to further investigate the selectivity. Compared with the case of only AA present (Fig. 5 ), the fluorescence intensity and absorbance intensity of the sensor system were not significantly affected by the presence of multiple interfering substances(arginine (Arg), histidine (His), serine (Ser), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Trp), Cu2+, Al3+, Na+, Mg2+, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), and sucrose). Hence, the sensing system shows remarkable selectivity and specificity with AA. The high selectivity of the CD-LEuH@MnO2 sensor for AA originates from three synergistic mechanisms: First, the enediol structure of AA exhibits significantly stronger reducing capability than other interfering substances (e.g., amino acids). Second, the lamellar structure of CD-LEuH@MnO2 enables free diffusion of small AA molecules to the active sites of MnO2, while large-molecule interferents (e.g., SDS, Trp) are excluded due to steric hindrance. Third, the CDs embedded within LEuH interlayers are protected by the host framework, thereby preventing interference from external substances that could compromise CD fluorescence.

In summary, a novel dual-mode sensor based on CD-LEuH@MnO2 was fabricated for the detection of AA. MnO2 in CD-LEuH@MnO2 can be decomposed by AA, which leads to the release of CD-LEuH, and the fluorescence enhancement and the decrease of absorbance can be observed. With the increase in AA concentration, the fluorescence and UV signals, being two independent signals, manifested a good linear relationship. The LOD was 0.081 μM for the fluorescence method and 0.064 μM for the colorimetric method. In addition, the CD-LEuH@MnO2 showed good performance in terms of practical samples and anti-interference ability. Therefore, this work provides a new idea for exploring multifunctional sensing nanomaterials.

Xinyu Er: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qingyang Gu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zhaoqian Niu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation. Jia Sun: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Zijun Ding: Validation, Formal analysis. Shuang Liang: Validation, Software. Haibo Jin: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2232051), and the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by BAST (BYESS2023258).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2025.114880.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xinyu Er is a graduate student at Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology. Her current research is fluorescence sensors.

Qingyang Gu is an associated professor at College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, China. Her research interests include preparation of functional nanomaterials, and fabrication of fluorescence sensors.
Zhaoqian Niu is an undergraduate student at Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology. His current research is fluorescence nanoprobes.
JiaSun is a graduate student at Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology. His current research is inorganic layered materials.
ZijunDing is a graduate student at Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology. His current research is electrochemical sensors.
ShuangLiang is a graduate student at Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology. Her current research is fluorescence sensors.

Haibo Jin is a professor at College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, China. His research interests include multiphase flow reaction and separation engineering.
